SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper supports its main empirical claims with extensive experiments on 22 STS datasets and supervised downstream tasks (see Algorithm 1, Table 1 and the experimental sections), and uses reasonable baselines (avg‑GloVe, tfidf, PSL, several supervised RNN/LSTM models). The mathematical derivation (Section 3, equations (2)–(3)) plausibly leads to the a/(a+p(w)) weighting, but relies on key approximations — notably that the partition function Zc̃s is roughly constant and a first‑order Taylor expansion — that are stated but not rigorously bounded, so the theoretical argument is heuristic rather than fully formal. The link to word2vec sub‑sampling (Section 3.1) is well motivated via expectations of the sampled gradient, though it glosses over bias/variance consequences of the biased estimator. Experimental methodology is broadly sound (parameter sensitivity, multiple corpora for p(w)), but no formal significance testing is reported. Overall the conclusions are supported, with minor technical gaps in the theoretical rigor and statistical reporting that do not overturn the empirical findings.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper contributes a simple, practical unsupervised sentence embedding (SIF weighting plus common component removal) and gives a novel generative‑model justification by extending the Arora et al. random‑walk discourse model (Section 3). Connecting this derived weighting to word2vec sub‑sampling (Section 3.1) and showing strong empirical results without paraphrase supervision is a notable contribution. The ideas reuse and extend prior models (Arora et al. 2016, Wieting et al. 2016) rather than introducing an entirely new framework, so the work is an original and nontrivial refinement rather than a radical departure.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized (intro, related work, model, algorithm, experiments, appendix) and states the main contributions early (intro, Algorithm 1). Notation is mostly consistent and the key steps of the derivation are presented clearly (equations (1)–(3)), and experimental settings and ablations are described in detail (A.1–A.3). A few approximations are invoked without detailed bounds and the provided excerpt contains some formatting/repetition artifacts, but overall the method, algorithm, and empirical evaluation are communicated clearly and reproducibly.
